task: I’ve got a utility issue and I don’t want to assume they’ll handle it right the first time. What should I keep an eye on from the first call through the final bill so I don’t get stuck chasing them later?

prefs.explanation: He seems unlikely to appreciate hedging, fluff, or diplomatic softening. His distrustful, confrontational style suggests he prefers answers that say exactly what they mean. Indirect wording would probably read to him as evasive or manipulative.

He values control and dislikes being dragged through unnecessary explanation. A concise response fits his impatient, self-protective style and respects that he wants the point quickly. Long-winded help would likely feel intrusive or wasteful to him.

He handles tasks by checking steps and verifying details, so organized output is likely the easiest for him to use. Clean structure helps him judge what matters without reading around the edges. Loose prose would be less useful than a direct, itemized format.

He does not seem to want warmth or emotional framing from strangers. A plain, steady tone matches his guarded style and avoids sounding patronizing. Overly cheerful or sympathetic help would probably irritate him.

prefs.short: Wants to approve big moves before acting
prefs.explanation: He is highly protective of control and dislikes people taking liberties on his behalf. That makes it likely he wants the assistant to pause before any significant step rather than assuming permission. He would probably dislike being surprised by action taken without explicit sign-off.

turns.agent_response:
Track it in three stages: **call, service, bill**.

## 1) First call
Write down:
- date/time
- name or ID of the person
- what you reported
- what they promised
- reference number / ticket number

Ask:
- when it will be handled
- whether a truck roll or inspection is needed
- whether there may be charges
- what you should expect next

## 2) During service
Keep notes on:
- who showed up
- what they actually did
- whether they changed a meter, seal, valve, line, or equipment
- whether they said anything about estimated billing or follow-up work

Take photos if there’s visible equipment, damage, or a meter issue.

## 3) Final bill
Check:
- service dates match what happened
- any one-time fees are explained
- estimated vs actual reads
- rate changes
- duplicate charges
- whether the promised fix actually stopped the problem

## Red flags
- no ticket number
- no written confirmation
- “we’ll look into it” with no follow-up date
- billing changes that don’t match the service visit
- repeated estimated bills after an actual meter read should have been possible

## What to save
- screenshots
- emails/texts
- photos
- bills
- names and dates of every contact

## Simple rule
If it isn’t in writing, act like it may disappear later.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page utility call checklist**.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "Track it in three stages" and "If it isn’t in writing, act like it may disappear later." No hedging or softening.
Fairly concise and efficient, with a compact checklist format. It’s not ultra-short, but it stays focused and avoids unnecessary explanation.
Uses clear bullets and numbered sections: "1) First call," "2) During service," "3) Final bill," plus "Red flags" and "What to save."
Matter-of-fact, unsentimental tone throughout. Phrases like "Track it" and "act like it may disappear later" are plain and practical, not warm or emotional.
Strongly supports approval/control before action: it tells the user to ask questions up front and to verify before relying on anything. It does not assume permission to act on the user’s behalf.

turns.agent_response:
Keep it in **three checkpoints**: **first call, service visit, final bill**.

## 1) First call — capture the facts
Write down:
- date/time
- utility name
- person’s name/ID
- **ticket/reference number**
- exact problem reported
- what they promised
- **date/time they say they’ll act**
- whether they said **estimated** or **actual** read
- whether they mentioned **any fee**

Ask:
- “What exactly happens next?”
- “Is there any charge for this?”
- “Will this be an estimated or actual meter read?”
- “When should I expect the fix?”

## 2) Service visit — verify what they actually did
Record:
- who showed up
- arrival/departure time
- what they changed or inspected
- whether they fixed the cause or only patched it
- whether they said a follow-up was needed
- any equipment, meter, seal, valve, or line work

If anything is visible, take photos.

## 3) Final bill — compare to your notes
Check:
- service date matches the visit
- promised no-charge work is actually no-charge
- estimated vs actual read
- new fees or renamed fees
- duplicate charges
- charges for a visit that didn’t solve the problem
- whether the next bill changed the way they said it would

## Biggest red flags
- no ticket number
- no named contact
- vague promise like “we’ll look into it”
- estimated bill when actual read should have been possible
- fee appears that nobody mentioned
- problem “fixed” but the bill doesn’t reflect it

## Keep
- screenshots
- bills
- photos
- emails/texts
- names, dates, ticket numbers

## Simple rule
If it isn’t in writing, assume you may have to prove it later.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page phone checklist** you can keep next to the bill.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "Keep it in three checkpoints" and "Simple rule" with no hedging or fluff.
Fairly concise and efficient, organized into short sections; not ultra-minimal, but avoids long explanation.
Uses clear bullets and checklist structure throughout: "Record:", "Ask:", "Check:", "Biggest red flags".
Mostly matter-of-fact and unsentimental, with plain instructions like "Record" and "Check"; only mild conversational softness in "If you want, I can turn this into..."
Respects approval/control by framing advice as what to watch for rather than acting on the user's behalf, and ends with "If you want" before offering a next step.
